text: Some of the innovations I will be presenting in this video is AHICH, antigenogenostomy technique, and tape measure.
The surgeon begins by dissecting the hiatus, preparing the area for the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon initiates perigastric dissection on the lesser curvature of the stomach, using blunt dissection to enter the lesser sac.
The surgeon inserts a 60mm blue load stapler through the right anterior 12mm port and fires it in a horizontal fashion, likely creating a staple line visible on the gastric tissue.
The surgeon completes the posterior pouch dissection, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the gastric bypass procedure.
The surgeon inserts a second blue load stapler through the twelve millimeter port at the left midclavicular line, firing it in a vertical fashion parallel to the lesser curve of the stomach.
Occasionally, I will use the Argyle tool for calibration.
The surgeon uses the second blue load to reach the angle of His, completing the gastric pouch. The ligament of Treitz is identified, and the proximal jejunum is measured.

The surgeon measures and brings up the proximal jejunum in an omega loop configuration. A window is created in the mesentery, and a four-inch Penrose drain with two slits is introduced. The loop is passed through the proximal slit, and the Penrose is secured to the liver retractor.
The surgeon performs a gastrotomy at the tip of the gastric pouch, creating a matching enterotomy in the adjacent small bowel.

A 60mm blue load stapler is introduced from the left side and partially fired, creating a staple line that is initially three centimeters long. After closure of the gastroenterotomy, the staple line narrows to two to two and a half centimeters. The hitch is removed, and the surgical window is revised.
minimum, if any, candy cane.
A white surgical stapler is introduced from the left side and positioned vertically, parallel to the existing anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the gastrointestinal anastomosis in two layers using a running 3-0 V-Loc absorbable suture, without placing a tube across the anastomosis at this stage.

Sometimes I will advance the tube if I have doubt or pass it before second layer. Same suture is run backwards. Sometimes one suture is enough to run both layers. Most of the time, two switchers are required. These are fifteen centimeter long switchers. And I will change the direction as needed if it is convenient.

The surgeon begins the second suture line, incorporating the staple line to cover the first inner line. The suture starts at the corner to ensure no gaps are left, overlapping with the first stitch. Although the barb suture does not require tying, the surgeon throws a couple of knots for added security.
The surgeon passes a tube across the anastomosis to ensure the posterior wall is not caught, then uses a six-inch tape measure to measure the length of the small bowel against it.

The surgeon measures the bowel using a suture, counting each throw of the fifteen-centimeter-long suture to reach approximately one hundred and fifty centimeters, ensuring the bowel is not stretched during the process.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors to make incisions at the biliopancreatic limb and the gastric pouch, creating an anastomosis site.

The surgeon introduces a 60mm wide stapler load from the left side, moving in an inferior to superior direction away from the staple end of the biliopancreatic limb. This maneuver aims to prevent narrowing near the jejunal jejunostomy at the glulam side, which can lead to twisting or complications in that area.
The surgeon closes a distal defect using sutures, demonstrating various techniques including horizontal, vertical, and longitudinal suturing, all effectively preventing angulation issues.

The surgeon sutures the blind end of the biliopancreatic limb using trio silk in Roland's fashion, ensuring the closure of the defect. Silk is chosen for its ability to create sufficient adhesions over time.

Again, one should be careful in closing these defects to make sure you don't create angulation which can then cause partial or complete obstruction at the jejun or jejunostomy, one of the dreaded complications of this anastomosis.

The surgeon closes the pseudo-Peterson space using a two-row ethobon suture in a purse-string fashion, incorporating part of the omentum and appendageal fat. The closure is limited to the lower portion, where hernias typically occur, without extending to the colon.
